calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion For Ruling On Verified Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus
Off Calendar Jun-05-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Atty. Lina Balciunas Cockrell of Messing Adam & Jasmine LLP, Tel. No. 1-916-446-5297 appearing for the Petitioners;
Atty. Dorothy Bacskai Egel of the State Personnel Board, Tel. No. 1-916-653-1466
appearing for the Respondent;
Atty. David M. Villalba of the Labor Relations Counsel, Tel. No. 1-916-324-0512
appearing for the Real Party in Interest CA Dept. of Forrestry & Fire Protection.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Petitioners Justin Chaplin, James Michels and Frank Schonig's petition for writ of administrative mandamus is denied. The precedential decisions of the California State Personnel Board on issues of general application within Board's constitutional authority are entitled to considerable deference per Yamaha Corp. of America v. State Bd. of Equalization (1998) 19 Cal.4th 1, 7. The double jeopardy rule invoked by petitioners is based on the Board's own precedential decisions and is not grounded in a constitutional or statutory provision. While petitioners' legal position is not unreasonable and is supported by considerations of fairness and public policy, the Board's position is neither a clearly erroneous nor arbitrary application of its precedents. The Board's determination that a withdrawal of an adverse action accompanied by backpay wipes the state clean and an employer is authorized to issue a second notice of adverse action based on substantially the same misconduct within the applicable limitations period and with the benefits of a new Skelly hearing without violating its own "double jeopardy" rule that an employee may not be disciplined twice for the same conduct is reasonably based on its own precedential decisions. (P.C. and S.W. (2006) SPB Dec. No. 06-01; Landeros (2004) SPB Dec.04-04; E.W. (1999) SPB Dec. No. 99-09). The Board's double jeopardy precedents are not based on Government Code 19575. The Board's determination that a second notice of adverse action based on substantially the same conduct as an earlier notice may be issued after discipline has become final on the first notice and then withdrawn does not run afoul of either section 19575 or any other statute or constitutional provision that has been cited by petitioners. Had the Legislature intended to bar the issuance of a second notice of adverse action after discipline based on the first notice became effective, it could have said so explicitly. There is no indication of any such intent in any of the statutes establishing the Board's procedures. While this court is sympathetic to the fairness concerns underlying the petition, fidelity to administrative law precedents and the limited scope of this court's review precludes the granting of the petition. The parties agreed to discard the administrative record lodged with the court. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Giselle Casey, CSR #8098 =302/hk
